symptomSummary: After a prompt revision accumulates ~30+ invocations, the evolve command ignores recent thumbs-down feedback and may produce worse revisions, resetting only when the revision is rolled back.
rootCauseHypothesis: The repository layer fetching invocation records for a given prompt revision likely lacks an ORDER BY recency + LIMIT clause, so as the total record count grows past ~30, recent negative signals are diluted or buried beneath older records before they reach the evolution logic.
fixHypothesis: Adding ORDER BY invoked_at DESC (or equivalent timestamp column) with a hard cap (e.g. LIMIT 20–50 most-recent records) to the invocation-fetch query would ensure the evolution agent always sees the freshest feedback regardless of total history depth; alternatively, the evolution service could weight or filter records by recency after fetching.

primaryLocation.rationale: Threshold-shaped symptoms that appear only after records accumulate almost always live in the data-access layer. If the SELECT retrieving invocations for a revision omits ORDER BY timestamp DESC LIMIT N, the result set grows unboundedly and buries recent thumbs-down signals. The ~30-invocation threshold is consistent with the point where 1–2 recent failures become statistically invisible among dozens of older records.
primaryLocation.function: getInvocations (or equivalent method querying invocation history per revision)

candidateLocations.rationale: This service orchestrates what feedback is handed to the LLM. Even if the DB query returns all records, this layer might concatenate them without a recency sort, causing older failures to crowd out newer ones in the prompt sent to the model.

At 433 lines this is the largest core file and likely assembles the LLM prompt driving revision generation. If it concatenates all invocations without recency weighting or a sliding window, older feedback dominates and the model addresses stale issues.
candidateLocations.function: the function constructing the evolution prompt from invocation history

candidateLocations.rationale: If the invocations table has no indexed timestamp column, efficient ORDER BY recency is impossible, which may have led the author to omit the sort entirely.

The API route triggering evolution may pass query parameters (limit, since-timestamp) down to the service; if absent or hardcoded to undefined, the service receives unbounded history with no recency anchor.
